title: Ensure 'HTTP version' is set to '2.0' (if in use)

input_row.title: Ensure 'HTTP version' is set to '2.0' (if in use)
input_row.description: Periodically, newer versions are released for HTTP, either due to security flaws or to include additional functionalities. Using the latest HTTP version allows apps to take advantage of security fixes, if any, and/or new functionalities of the newer version.
input_row.rationale: Newer versions may contain security enhancements and additional functionalities. Using the latest version is recommended in order to take advantage of enhancements and new capabilities. With each software installation, organizations need to determine if a given update meets their requirements. They must also verify the compatibility and support provided for any additional software against the update revision that is selected. HTTP 2.0 has additional performance improvements for the head-of-line blocking problem of the old HTTP version, header compression, and request prioritization. HTTP 2.0 no longer supports HTTP 1.1's chunked transfer encoding mechanism, as it provides its own, more efficient mechanisms for data streaming.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services or Function App. 2. Click the name of a function app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Configuration. 6. In the General settings pane, under Platform settings, ensure that HTTP version is set to 2.0. 7. Repeat steps 1-6 for each function app and deployment slot. Audit from Azure CLI Run the following command to list function apps: az functionapp list For each function app, run the following command to list deployment slots: az functionapp deployment slot list --resource-group -- name For each deployment slot, run the following command to get the http20Enabled setting: az resource show --name web --resource-group -- namespace Microsoft.Web --resource-type config --parent sites//slots/ --query properties.http20Enabled Ensure that true is returned. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: fa98f1b1-1f56-4179-9faf-93ad82f3458f - Name: 'Function app slots should use latest 'HTTP Version''
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services or Function App. 2. Click the name of a function app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Configuration. 6. In the General settings pane, under Platform settings, set HTTP version to 2.0. 7. Click Save. 8. Click Continue. 9. Repeat steps 1-8 for each function app and deployment slot requiring remediation. Remediate from Azure CLI For each deployment slot requiring remediation, run the following command to enable http20Enabled: az resource update --name web --resource-group -- namespace Microsoft.Web --resource-type config --parent sites//slots/ --set properties.http20Enabled=true
input_row.impact: Most modern browsers support the HTTP/2 protocol over TLS only, while non-encrypted traffic continues to use HTTP/1.1. To ensure that client browsers connect to your app with HTTP/2, either purchase an App Service Certificate for your app's custom domain or bind a third-party certificate. NOTE: HTTP/2 cannot be used in tandem with mutual authentication or client certificates.
input_row.default_value: By default, HTTP version is set to 1.1.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_lambda_function_http_version_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program would check the following:

1. **AWS API Calls / Boto3 SDK Methods:**
   - Use `boto3` to access AWS Lambda function configurations. Since AWS Lambda supports HTTP through API Gateway or Application Load Balancer, it’s important to verify the HTTP version settings relevant to those resources.
   
2. **AWS CLI Commands (if applicable):**
   - To list Lambda functions:
     ```bash
     aws lambda list-functions
     ```
   - To fetch the configurations of a specific Lambda function (you can follow this with API Gateway or ALB commands):
     ```bash
     aws lambda get-function-configuration --function-name <your_function_name>
     ```

3. **Identify Relevant Resources:**
   - List all Lambda functions deployed within your AWS account in a specific region.
   - Examine associated API Gateway or Application Load Balancer settings, as they would be the endpoints serving HTTP requests.

4. **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
   - For API Gateway and Application Load Balancer, check if HTTP/2 is enabled:
     - For API Gateway, this can be fetched using:
       ```bash
       aws apigateway get-rest-apis
       ```
       Then validate each API if it enables HTTP/2.

5. **Clear Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - Pass: If the configured settings indicate that HTTP/2 is supported/enabled within the Lambda function’s API Gateway or ALB configuration.
   - Fail: If HTTP/2 is not enabled, indicating the use of an older version (HTTP/1.1).

6. **Example Criteria to verify:**
   - Ensure the API Gateway configuration has the attribute indicating HTTP/2 support.
   - For Application Load Balancer configuration, ensure that the listener settings have HTTP/2 set. 

This ensures continuous monitoring and compliance as per the security guidelines advocated in the source material referencing best practices for HTTP protocol versions within modern serverless architectures.
